relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Pilate hands Jesus to flogging.
Thorns and purple parody kingship.
Mock homage strikes the king.
Pilate again declares no cause.
Behold the human: wounded kingship exposed.
The demand becomes crucifixion despite Pilate’s no-cause verdict.
The accusation shifts to Son of the Divine.
Pilate’s fear increases.
Origin is questioned; Jesus is silent.
Pilate boasts of release and death authority.
Jesus locates authority as given from above.
Caesar-pressure corners Pilate.
Judgment seat and Stone Pavement become the stage.
At Passover Preparation, Pilate says, Behold your king.
No king but Caesar reveals the compromise.
Jesus is handed over for crucifixion.
He carries the cross to Skull Place.
He is crucified in the middle.
The title names him Jesus the Nazarene, King of the Judeans.
The title is public in three languages.
The chief priests object to the wording.
Pilate’s written word remains written.
The garments are divided, the seamless tunic kept whole.
Lots over the clothing fulfill Scripture.
The women stand by the cross.
Jesus gives his mother and beloved disciple to each other.
A new household is formed from the cross.
Knowing completion, Jesus says, I thirst.
Sour wine is lifted on hyssop.
It is completed; he hands over the spirit.
Preparation and Sabbath urgency shape the aftermath.
The soldiers break the legs of the others.
Jesus’ legs are not broken.
Blood and water flow from the pierced side.
The seeing witness speaks truth so trust may awaken.
No broken bone fulfills Scripture.
They look on the pierced one.
Joseph, hidden disciple, asks for the body.
Nicodemus returns from night with burial spices.
The body is wrapped with linen and spices.
A garden near the cross holds a new tomb.
Because the tomb is near, Jesus is laid there.
